title: SQL
purpose: Model relational data and write correct, performant queries for application and analytics work.
outcomes: Explain SQL concepts from first principles
Choose when to use and when to avoid important SQL features
Implement, test and debug practical SQL solutions
Answer conceptual, internal-working and production interview questions

stages.title: Fundamentals
stages.objective: Build an accurate mental model of SQL before relying on frameworks or memorized answers.

topics.title: Tables, rows and data types
topics.definition: SQL is a declarative language for defining, querying and changing relational data while the database optimizer chooses an execution plan.
topics.whyItMatters: It expresses data relationships, constraints and set-based transformations close to durable storage.
whenToUse: Model data with strong relationships and transactional invariants
Join, aggregate and rank related records
Let constraints reject invalid data regardless of application path
whenNotToUse: Fetching all rows to filter in application memory
Using SELECT * for stable production contracts
Adding indexes without understanding read and write paths
internalWorking: Parsing and binding resolve names and types
The optimizer estimates row counts and compares access paths
The executor scans indexes or tables and applies joins, filters and aggregates
Transactions coordinate visibility and durability through locks or multiversion mechanisms
tradeoffs: Normalization protects consistency but may require joins
Indexes accelerate selected reads but consume space and slow writes
Stronger isolation prevents anomalies but can reduce concurrency
commonMistakes: Filtering a LEFT JOINed table in WHERE and accidentally making it inner
Ignoring NULL three-valued logic
Paginating large changing data with deep OFFSET
topics.practicalExample: Group students by section, calculate placement rate with conditional aggregation, and index the foreign key used to join students to sections.
topics.code:
SELECT section_id, COUNT(*) AS students,
       AVG(CASE WHEN placed THEN 1.0 ELSE 0 END) AS placement_rate
FROM students
GROUP BY section_id
ORDER BY placement_rate DESC;

topics.title: Locking and concurrency
topics.definition: Concurrency coordinates multiple tasks whose lifetimes overlap. Parallelism executes work simultaneously; asynchronous I/O lets a task yield while waiting for external work.
topics.whyItMatters: Applications must remain responsive while handling slow networks, files, user requests and CPU work without corrupting shared state.
whenToUse: Use async I/O for many independent network or disk waits
Use bounded worker pools for controlled parallel work
Protect shared mutable state with ownership, immutability or synchronization
whenNotToUse: Adding threads to CPU work without measuring cores and overhead
Blocking an event-loop thread
Holding a lock while performing slow external I/O
internalWorking: A scheduler chooses which runnable task gets CPU time
Async runtimes register I/O readiness and resume continuations later
Threads have independent stacks but share process memory
Synchronization establishes ordering and visibility for shared state
tradeoffs: More throughput versus scheduling and coordination overhead
Shared memory is fast but increases race risk
Async code scales I/O waits but can make cancellation and error propagation harder
commonMistakes: Confusing concurrency with parallelism
Launching unbounded work
Assuming a single statement is automatically thread-safe
topics.practicalExample: Fetch independent question sets concurrently with a timeout, but limit concurrency so one user cannot exhaust database or network connections.

       AVG(CASE WHEN placed THEN 1.0 ELSE 0 END) AS placement_rate

GROUP BY section_id
ORDER BY placement_rate DESC;

topics.title: Transactions protect invariants
topics.definition: Semantic HTML uses elements according to their meaning so browsers and assistive technology can provide correct structure and interaction behavior.
topics.whyItMatters: A visual interface alone does not communicate roles, names, relationships or keyboard behavior to every user and device.
whenToUse: Use headings and landmarks to describe document structure
Use native buttons, links, inputs and labels for interaction
Use ARIA only to fill a semantic gap that native HTML cannot cover
whenNotToUse: Clickable div elements
ARIA roles that conflict with native behavior
Placeholder text as the only form label
internalWorking: The browser parses markup into the DOM
Semantics contribute to the accessibility tree
Native controls expose keyboard, focus and state behavior
Labels create an accessible name used by assistive technology
tradeoffs: Native controls are robust but may require deliberate styling
Custom widgets offer unique behavior but carry full accessibility responsibility
Extra markup can aid structure but meaningless wrappers add noise
commonMistakes: Skipping heading levels for appearance
Using a button for navigation or a link for an action
Adding tabindex to every element
topics.practicalExample: Use a form with an associated email label, native validation and a submit button; announce server errors beside the field and move focus only when necessary.
